items.q: What can line in a composition imply, even without representing an object?
items.correct: Movement, direction, emotion, and visual rhythm.
items.misconception: Only the outline of an object.
items.why: Students treat line as merely descriptive (an outline). In design, line is expressive - jagged lines suggest energy, curves softness, diagonals movement, even abstractly.

items.correct: Visual equilibrium achieved with different elements of equal visual weight on each side.
items.why: Students confuse asymmetry with imbalance. Asymmetrical balance is still BALANCE - achieved through unequal but visually weighted elements (e.g., a small dark shape balancing a large light one).

items.correct: The small projecting line at the end of letter strokes.
items.misconception: A whole typeface that looks formal.
items.why: Students conflate the FEATURE (the small foot/stroke) with the CLASSIFICATION (serif typefaces, which have them). Sans serif means 'without serifs' - those little projections at letter endings.

items.q: Which contemporary British ceramicist is known for the 'Vase of Tears' and Turner Prize win in 2003?
items.why: Correct answer: Grayson Perry. “Edmund de Waal” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which discipline focuses on shaping the inside of buildings?
items.why: It's Interior design. “Architecture” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year did Grayson Perry win the Turner Prize?
items.why: The real figure is 2003. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
